meanings.definition: To imitate someone or something, especially out of admiration and a desire to match or surpass it.
examples: Younger players try to emulate the star's technique.
She emulated the writers she most admired.

meanings.definition: The crime of marrying someone while already legally married to another person.
examples: He was charged with bigamy after his first marriage came to light.
Bigamy is illegal in most countries.

meanings.definition: To do arithmetic; to calculate with numbers.
examples: The old clerk could cipher long columns of figures in his head.

meanings.definition: A secret way of writing, or the symbol zero.
examples: The spy sent the message in a cipher only she could read.

meanings.definition: Being in complete agreement and harmony.
examples: The choir's voices were unisonant, blending into a single sound.
Their views were so unisonant that no debate was needed.

meanings.definition: Seeming reasonable or likely to be true, though not certain.
examples: She offered a plausible explanation for her absence.
The theory sounds plausible, but it still needs proof.

meanings.definition: The act of cutting off, as in a surgical procedure.
examples: The surgeon performed a clean abscission of the damaged tissue.

meanings.definition: The natural shedding of leaves, flowers, or fruit from a plant.
examples: In autumn, abscission causes the leaves to fall from the trees.

meanings.definition: A hollow cavity or opening, especially one of the air-filled spaces in the bones of the face.
examples: A bad cold left her sinuses completely blocked.
The infection had spread into the nasal sinus.

meanings.definition: A young swan.
examples: A single cygnet followed its mother across the pond.
The grey cygnet had not yet grown its white feathers.

meanings.definition: Present or found everywhere at the same time.
examples: Smartphones have become ubiquitous in modern life.
Coffee shops are ubiquitous in the city center.

meanings.definition: Something made to look like or copy something else.
examples: The necklace was a cheap imitation of the real diamond.
His painting is a skillful imitation of the old master's style.

meanings.definition: Cowardly and contemptible.
examples: The caitiff soldier fled the moment the battle began.
She despised his caitiff refusal to defend his friend.

meanings.definition: Willingly and without hesitation or reluctance.
examples: He readily agreed to help with the project.

meanings.definition: Easily and without difficulty.
examples: The information is readily available online.

meanings.definition: The point where two rivers or streams flow together.
examples: The city was built at the confluence of two great rivers.

meanings.definition: A coming together of people or things.
examples: The festival was a confluence of music, art, and food.

meanings.definition: A full or abundant supply of something.
examples: The garden offered a plenitude of fresh vegetables all summer.
There was a plenitude of opportunities for the graduates.

meanings.definition: To formally propose someone as a candidate for a position or honor.
examples: The committee nominated her for the leadership role.
He was nominated for an award for his research.

meanings.definition: Appearing true or convincing but actually false or misleading.
examples: His argument was specious and fell apart under scrutiny.
She saw through the salesman's specious promises.

meanings.definition: A formal agreement or treaty between people or parties.
examples: The two nations signed a peace pact.
They made a pact never to reveal the secret.

meanings.definition: Forbidden by law or rules; unlawful.
examples: The police seized a large quantity of illicit drugs.
They were involved in illicit trade across the border.

meanings.definition: Lying or extending across something in a crosswise direction.
examples: A transverse beam supported the width of the roof.
The doctor took a transverse section of the sample.

meanings.definition: Showing deep, careful study and knowledge; characteristic of a learned person.
examples: She wrote a scholarly article on medieval poetry.
His scholarly approach impressed the entire committee.

meanings.definition: The act of driving back an attacking or advancing force.
examples: The repulse of the enemy assault saved the fortress.
After the repulse, the troops regrouped behind the wall.

meanings.definition: To go before something as an introduction or sign of its coming.
examples: Dark clouds forerun the approaching storm.
These early symptoms often forerun the full illness.

meanings.definition: To confirm or declare that something is true, genuine, or accurate.
examples: Witnesses attested to the accuracy of her account.
His success attests to years of hard work.

meanings.definition: Something added, especially extra material appended to a document or book.
examples: The contract included an addendum covering late payments.
She wrote an addendum to clarify the earlier report.

meanings.definition: The total formed by combining several separate elements or amounts.
examples: In the aggregate, the donations exceeded a million dollars.
The score is based on the aggregate of all the rounds.

meanings.definition: Impossible to grasp or understand.
examples: The ancient script remained inapprehensible to modern scholars.
His reasoning was so tangled as to be inapprehensible.

meanings.definition: The branch of science concerned with the bodily structure of living things.
examples: Medical students spend months studying human anatomy.

meanings.definition: The internal structure or makeup of something.
examples: The book examines the anatomy of a successful business.

meanings.definition: To make a problem or condition worse.
examples: Scratching the bite will only aggravate the irritation.
The new policy aggravated an already tense situation.

meanings.definition: To annoy or irritate someone.
examples: His constant tapping began to aggravate his coworkers.

meanings.definition: Having ten feet, legs, or arms.
examples: Crabs and lobsters are decapod crustaceans.
The decapod creature gripped the rock with its many limbs.

meanings.definition: A state of balance between opposing forces or influences.
examples: The scales reached equilibrium when the weights were equal.
After the crisis, the market returned to equilibrium.

meanings.definition: The act of transferring blood or fluid from one body or container into another.
examples: The patient needed a blood transfusion after the accident.
A timely transfusion saved the injured climber's life.

meanings.definition: A person or thing that serves as a model or perfect example.
examples: She is held up as an exemplar of hard work and honesty.
The cathedral is an exemplar of Gothic architecture.

meanings.definition: Going on without any break or interruption.
examples: The machine emitted a continuous hum all night.
There was a continuous flow of traffic on the highway.

meanings.definition: Extremely impressive, large, or great in size or degree.
examples: They made a stupendous effort to finish on time.
The view from the summit was absolutely stupendous.

meanings.definition: Willing to please others and agreeable in manner.
examples: The complaisant host agreed to every request from his guests.
She was too complaisant to refuse the extra work.

meanings.definition: To cause someone to do or believe something through reasoning or argument.
examples: She persuaded her parents to let her travel abroad.
No amount of evidence could persuade him to change his mind.

meanings.definition: Something that marks or expresses a beginning; in grammar, a verb form showing the start of an action.
examples: The verb 'to dawn' functions as an inchoative, signaling something beginning.
Linguists classify such forms as inchoatives.

meanings.definition: A false or pretended apostle.
examples: The early church warned its members against any pseudapostle.
He was denounced as a pseudapostle spreading false teachings.

meanings.definition: To ask a question or express a doubt about something.
examples: She queried whether the figures were correct.
He queried the waiter about the unfamiliar dish.

meanings.definition: A great difference or inequality between things.
examples: There is a wide disparity between the rich and the poor.
The disparity in their salaries caused resentment.

meanings.definition: A renewal or restoration of youth and vitality.
examples: The spa promised a feeling of rejuvenescence after each visit.
Spring brought a sense of rejuvenescence to the whole valley.

meanings.definition: A unit of energy, especially as a measure of the energy that food provides.
examples: This snack contains about two hundred calories.
She counts every calorie to stay within her diet.

meanings.definition: In a direct and straightforward manner.
examples: He answered forthright, without any evasion.
She spoke forthright about the company's problems.

meanings.definition: To stop or catch something or someone before they reach their destination.
examples: The goalkeeper intercepted the pass before it reached the striker.
Agents intercepted the message before it could be sent.

meanings.definition: A small group of troops or a station positioned at a distance to guard against surprise attack.
examples: The scouts manned a lonely outpost on the frontier.

meanings.definition: A remote settlement or branch far from the main center.
examples: The trading company built an outpost deep in the wilderness.

meanings.definition: The state of being equal, especially in status, rank, or amount.
examples: The union demanded pay parity with workers at other firms.
The two currencies reached parity for the first time in years.

meanings.definition: To make someone or something unable to move or act.
examples: The injury paralyzed his left arm.
Fear seemed to paralyze her completely.

meanings.definition: Deep wisdom, insight, or good judgment.
examples: The elder was admired for his quiet sapience.
Her advice reflected a rare sapience born of experience.

meanings.definition: The fact or amount of difference or change between things.
examples: There was a noticeable variance between the two reports.
The recipe allows for some variance in the cooking time.

meanings.definition: A disagreement or conflict.
examples: His actions were at variance with his stated beliefs.

meanings.definition: Showing strong dislike or lack of respect; scornful.
examples: She gave a contemptuous laugh at his excuse.
He was contemptuous of anyone who disagreed with him.

meanings.definition: A distinguishing feature or quality of a person or thing.
examples: Generosity was her most admirable trait.
Curiosity is a common trait among young children.

meanings.definition: Of a bright but delicate yellow color.
examples: She wore a canary dress that stood out in the crowd.
The walls were painted a cheerful canary yellow.

meanings.definition: A small songbird often kept as a pet for its yellow color and singing.
examples: The canary chirped happily in its cage.

meanings.definition: Forbidding or restricting something, especially by law, such as the sale of alcohol.
examples: The town passed prohibitory laws against selling liquor on Sundays.
The fees were so high as to be prohibitory for most families.

meanings.definition: Expressing protest, reproof, or objection.
examples: He raised a remonstrant hand to stop the noisy debate.
Her remonstrant tone made clear she disapproved of the plan.

meanings.definition: A short interval of rest or relief from something difficult or unpleasant.
examples: The cool evening brought a welcome respite from the heat.
Workers were given a brief respite before the next shift.

meanings.definition: The death of body tissue or part of the body, usually from disease or injury.
examples: The doctor warned that necrosis could set in if blood flow was not restored.
Tissue necrosis around the wound required surgical removal.

meanings.definition: A supplement or addition to a will that adds to, revokes, or explains part of it.
examples: She added a codicil to her will leaving her jewelry to her niece.
A codicil was attached to amend the original terms of the estate.

meanings.definition: A sudden, violent outburst of emotion or activity.
examples: He was seized by a paroxysm of laughter he could not control.
The crowd erupted in a paroxysm of rage at the news.

meanings.definition: A sudden recurrence or intensification of symptoms, such as a fit or convulsion.
examples: The patient suffered a paroxysm of coughing during the night.

meanings.definition: Effective in producing a desired or intended result.
examples: The new medicine proved highly efficacious against the infection.
Their efforts were efficacious in raising money for the school.

meanings.definition: Relating to or characterized by moving from one region or habitat to another.
examples: Many migratory birds fly south for the winter.
The tribe led a migratory life, following the herds across the plains.

meanings.definition: In Catholic belief, an intermediate state where souls are purified through suffering before entering heaven.
examples: The poem describes the soul's journey through purgatory toward paradise.

meanings.definition: A place or state of temporary suffering or misery.
examples: The long delay at the airport felt like pure purgatory.

meanings.definition: A storehouse or building for keeping threshed grain.
examples: The farmers filled the granary with wheat after the harvest.
Rats had gotten into the granary and spoiled some of the grain.

meanings.definition: Founded on truth or sound reasoning; well grounded.
examples: She made a valid point that no one could dispute.
His argument was logical and valid.

meanings.definition: Legally or officially acceptable and in force.
examples: Your passport is valid for another five years.

meanings.definition: To be the greatest in number, importance, or influence; to prevail.
examples: Bright colors predominate in her latest paintings.
Caution tends to predominate when investors are nervous.

meanings.definition: The exercise of absolute power, especially in a cruel or oppressive way.
examples: The people rose up against the despotism of the king.
Under his despotism, no citizen dared to speak freely.

meanings.definition: A person or thing of unequaled excellence; something without rival.
examples: As a violinist she was the nonpareil of her generation.
This wine is regarded as the nonpareil of the region.

meanings.definition: Lacking moral restraint; given to indulgence and loose living.
examples: He led a dissolute life of gambling and drinking.
The novel portrays the dissolute habits of the idle rich.

meanings.definition: Difficulty or discomfort in digesting food.
examples: The rich meal gave him terrible indigestion.
She took an antacid to relieve her indigestion.

meanings.definition: Unwilling to consider views or ideas different from one's own.
examples: His narrow-minded attitude made it hard to discuss new ideas with him.
It was narrow-minded of them to reject the proposal without listening.

meanings.definition: A sudden and violent disruption of an established order or condition.
examples: The revolution caused a massive upheaval across the country.
The merger led to upheaval within the company.

meanings.definition: A forceful upward movement of part of the earth's surface.
examples: The mountain range was formed by a great geological upheaval.

meanings.definition: Relating to the tongue, especially its use in speech.
examples: Certain lingual sounds are difficult for beginners to pronounce.
The speech therapist focused on his lingual movements.

meanings.definition: Behaving as though one is superior to others; haughtily contemptuous.
examples: The waiter gave us a supercilious smile when we asked about prices.
Her supercilious manner annoyed everyone in the meeting.

meanings.definition: The order in which persons, things, or events follow one another in space or time.
examples: The events are described in the sequence in which they occurred.
She memorized the sequence of steps for the experiment.

meanings.definition: A set of related things that follow each other in a particular order.
examples: The film opens with a thrilling chase sequence.

meanings.definition: Relating to the study or cultivation of plants.
examples: We spent the afternoon at the botanical garden.
Her botanical drawings captured every detail of the leaves.

meanings.definition: To deny responsibility for or connection with something.
examples: The party moved quickly to disavow the controversial remarks.
He disavowed any knowledge of the missing funds.

meanings.definition: Having a rough or harsh voice, as from a cold, shouting, or fatigue.
examples: His voice was hoarse after cheering at the game all night.
She spoke in a hoarse whisper because of her sore throat.

meanings.definition: A deep, narrow gorge or valley, especially one worn by running water.
examples: The hikers descended carefully into the rocky ravine.
A stream rushed through the bottom of the ravine.

meanings.definition: Belonging to a grammatical gender that is neither masculine nor feminine.
examples: In German, the word for child takes the neuter gender.
Many English nouns are treated as neuter.

meanings.definition: To remove the reproductive organs of an animal; to spay or castrate.
examples: The vet recommended that we neuter our new kitten.

meanings.definition: Deserving or causing public disgrace or shame.
examples: The team suffered an ignominious defeat in the final.
His ignominious exit from office made headlines.

meanings.definition: Lacking the power to move or act; inanimate.
examples: He lay inert on the couch, too tired to move.
The drug left the patient completely inert for hours.

meanings.definition: Chemically inactive; not reacting readily with other substances.
examples: Helium is an inert gas that does not burn.

meanings.definition: A mark of honor or recognition awarded for excellence or success.
examples: She graduated with distinction in mathematics.
The award is a rare distinction for someone so young.

meanings.definition: A difference or contrast between similar things.
examples: He drew a clear distinction between fact and opinion.

meanings.definition: First in rank or importance; foremost.
examples: She was the prima ballerina of the company.
The prima soprano received the loudest applause.

meanings.definition: A group of buildings forming the home of a community of monks or nuns, headed by an abbot or abbess.
examples: The monks have lived in the abbey for centuries.
We toured the ruins of the medieval abbey.

meanings.definition: A person who enjoys fine food and drink, sometimes to excess.
examples: As a gourmand, he traveled the world in search of the best meals.
The gourmand savored every course of the elaborate feast.

meanings.definition: To admire or love someone or something greatly, often excessively.
examples: Many young athletes idolize their favorite players.
She idolized her older sister and copied everything she did.

meanings.definition: A written or printed copy made directly from an original.
examples: The reporter checked the transcript of the interview.
He requested a transcript of the court proceedings.

meanings.definition: An official record of a student's coursework and grades.
examples: She sent her college transcript to the admissions office.

meanings.definition: The act of rising, climbing, or moving upward.
examples: The ascent to the summit took six hours.
The balloon's slow ascent thrilled the watching children.

meanings.definition: An upward slope or path.
examples: The final ascent of the trail was steep and rocky.

meanings.definition: Relating to money or the currency of a country.
examples: The bank announced a change in monetary policy.
The painting has great sentimental as well as monetary value.

meanings.definition: To burst or cause to burst violently as a result of internal pressure or force.
examples: The bomb exploded with a deafening roar.
Engineers worried the boiler might explode.

meanings.definition: To suddenly express strong emotion, especially anger.
examples: He exploded in fury when he heard the news.

meanings.definition: To accept or comply with something, often reluctantly and without protest.
examples: She finally acquiesced to her parents' wishes.
Rather than argue, he acquiesced in the committee's decision.

meanings.definition: Keeping careful watch for possible danger or difficulties.
examples: The guards remained vigilant throughout the night.
Parents must be vigilant about what their children watch online.

meanings.definition: To delay or postpone doing something, especially out of habit or reluctance.
examples: I always procrastinate when it comes to paying bills.
Don't procrastinate, or you'll never finish the project.

meanings.definition: Lacking the basic necessities of life; extremely poor.
examples: The war left thousands of families destitute.
After losing his job, he was nearly destitute.

meanings.definition: The official power or authority to make legal decisions and judgments.
examples: The case falls under the jurisdiction of the federal court.
The police had no jurisdiction outside the city limits.

meanings.definition: The territory or area within which authority may be exercised.
examples: The crime occurred outside their jurisdiction.

meanings.definition: The main course of a meal.
examples: For my entree I ordered the grilled salmon.
The restaurant offers several vegetarian entrees.

meanings.definition: The right, privilege, or means of entering or gaining admission.
examples: Her talent gave her entree into the world of fashion.

meanings.definition: To use up or drain something completely.
examples: They exhausted their savings within a year.
We had exhausted every possible option.

meanings.definition: To tire someone out completely.
examples: The long hike exhausted the children.

meanings.definition: Waste gases or air expelled from an engine.
examples: The car's exhaust filled the garage with fumes.

meanings.definition: Relating to the later part of the Stone Age, marked by polished stone tools and farming.
examples: The museum displays tools from the Neolithic period.
Neolithic peoples were among the first to farm the land.

meanings.definition: An outline of the topics and requirements of a course of study.
examples: The professor handed out the syllabus on the first day.
Shakespeare's plays are on this year's syllabus.

meanings.definition: A prolonged period of dry weather that causes vegetation to wither.
examples: The long drought ruined the season's crops.
Reservoirs ran low during the worst drought in decades.

meanings.definition: Capable of sinning or doing wrong.
examples: The sermon reminded the congregation that all humans are peccable.
Even saints are peccable in the eyes of strict theologians.

meanings.definition: A special right or advantage granted to a particular person or group.
examples: Education should be a right, not a privilege.
Members enjoy the privilege of early access to events.

meanings.definition: To grant a special right or advantage to someone.
examples: The system privileges the wealthy over the poor.

meanings.definition: Realistic; closely resembling a living person or thing.
examples: The wax figures were astonishingly lifelike.
The artist painted a lifelike portrait of the old man.

meanings.definition: A person who develops or is concerned with ideas and theories about a subject.
examples: She is a leading theorist in modern economics.
The political theorist published a book on democracy.

meanings.definition: The quality of being morally right, just, or virtuous.
examples: He was admired for his honesty and righteousness.
The preacher spoke about living a life of righteousness.

meanings.definition: Legally or justly responsible for something.
examples: The driver was held liable for the damage.
Both partners are liable for the company's debts.

meanings.definition: Likely to do or experience something undesirable.
examples: Without a coat you're liable to catch a cold.

meanings.definition: The way in which two or more things are not alike.
examples: There is a clear difference between the two designs.
Can you spot the difference between these pictures?

meanings.definition: A disagreement or dispute.
examples: They settled their differences and became friends again.

meanings.definition: A difficult, unpleasant, or embarrassing situation.
examples: He found himself in a real predicament with no money and no ride home.
The company is in a financial predicament.

meanings.definition: To arrange something again or in a different order.
examples: We rearranged the furniture to make the room feel larger.
She rearranged the schedule to fit in another meeting.

meanings.definition: To provide something helpful as a kindness or favor; to do a service for.
examples: The hotel staff did everything they could to accommodate us.
She rearranged her plans to accommodate her guests.

meanings.definition: To provide lodging or enough space for.
examples: The hall can accommodate up to five hundred people.

meanings.definition: To take off one's clothes; to undress.
examples: He disrobed and stepped into the shower.
The patient was asked to disrobe before the examination.

meanings.definition: Relating to typography, printing, or the arrangement of printed text.
examples: The book contained several typographical errors.
She studied the typographical style of old manuscripts.

meanings.definition: The quality of being diverse or made up of unlike parts.
examples: The heterogeneity of the population makes generalizations difficult.
Researchers noted the heterogeneity of the samples.

meanings.definition: A person who is excessively self-centered or who believes self-interest should guide action.
examples: As an egoist, he rarely considered anyone else's feelings.
The philosopher was a confirmed egoist in his ethics.

meanings.definition: A secluded dwelling or hermitage where a recluse lives.
examples: The monk withdrew to his reclusory in the hills.
The old reclusory had not been visited for years.

meanings.definition: Unable to be heard.
examples: Her reply was almost inaudible over the noise.
The whistle is inaudible to human ears.

meanings.definition: Unconventional and slightly strange in behavior or appearance.
examples: The eccentric inventor filled his house with odd machines.
She has an eccentric habit of talking to her plants.

meanings.definition: Not having the same center; off-center.
examples: The planet follows an eccentric orbit around the sun.

meanings.definition: Pleasant and agreeable because of having a similar character, tastes, or interests.
examples: They were congenial companions who shared the same hobbies.
The cafe had a warm, congenial atmosphere.

meanings.definition: To express or show intense, eager enthusiasm.
examples: She enthused about her trip to Italy for hours.
Critics enthused over the young director's first film.

meanings.definition: A state of complete disorder and confusion.
examples: The unexpected storm threw the airport into chaos.
After the power went out, the office descended into chaos.

meanings.definition: Recently hatched and not yet able to leave the nest.
examples: The nestling birds opened their beaks for food.
A tiny nestling chick peeked over the edge of the nest.

meanings.definition: A young bird too undeveloped to leave the nest.
examples: The cat had found a fallen nestling in the garden.

meanings.definition: The solid surface of the earth or a floor on which one stands or walks.
examples: The climbers were relieved to feel solid ground beneath their feet again.
She dropped her keys on the ground and bent to pick them up.

meanings.definition: A reason or basis for a belief, action, or argument.
examples: He was dismissed on the grounds of repeated absence.

meanings.definition: To prevent an aircraft or pilot from flying, or to base something on a firm foundation.
examples: All flights were grounded by the heavy snowstorm.

meanings.definition: Suitable or able to be received, especially money owed to a business.
examples: The accountant listed the receivable amounts in a separate column.
Payments still receivable from clients totaled over ten thousand dollars.

meanings.definition: An amount of money owed to a company by its customers.
examples: The firm sold off its receivables to improve its cash flow.

meanings.definition: To decrease gradually in size, strength, or brightness.
examples: The moon began to wane after the full moon had passed.
His enthusiasm for the project waned as the deadline slipped further away.

meanings.definition: Not existing or not real.
examples: The supposed treasure turned out to be entirely non-existent.
Their so-called evidence was practically non-existent.

meanings.definition: Behaving or done in a way that is deceitful and not to be trusted.
examples: The dishonest clerk was caught altering the receipts.
It would be dishonest to claim credit for work I never did.

meanings.definition: A senior non-commissioned officer who is the highest-ranking enlisted soldier in a unit.
examples: The sergeant-major barked orders across the parade ground.
Every recruit feared a reprimand from the sergeant-major.

meanings.definition: A warning or caution given as friendly advice or a gentle reproof.
examples: The teacher's monition reminded the students to study before the exam.
He ignored every monition until it was far too late.

meanings.definition: To comfort someone in a time of grief or disappointment.
examples: She tried to console her friend after the sad news.
Nothing could console the child who had lost her toy.

meanings.definition: To dull or destroy the shine of a metal surface, or to damage a reputation.
examples: The silver spoons had tarnished after years in the drawer.
The scandal tarnished the politician's once-spotless reputation.

meanings.definition: A person who operates or controls a machine, device, or system.
examples: The crane operator carefully lifted the steel beam into place.
A telephone operator connected the call within seconds.

meanings.definition: Having the power to loosen or stimulate the bowels.
examples: Prunes are known for their mild laxative effect.
The doctor prescribed a laxative diet to ease his discomfort.

meanings.definition: A food or medicine that stimulates bowel movements.
examples: She took a gentle laxative before the medical procedure.

meanings.definition: The branch of medicine dealing with the care and diseases of children.
examples: After medical school she specialized in pediatrics.
The hospital's pediatrics ward was painted with cheerful murals.

meanings.definition: A disease or disorder, especially a contagious viral illness affecting animals such as dogs.
examples: The puppy was vaccinated against distemper.
A strange distemper spread through the kennel that winter.

meanings.definition: Easily set on fire, or easily provoked to strong emotion.
examples: Gasoline is highly inflammable and must be stored with care.
His inflammable temper made arguments erupt over trifles.

meanings.definition: To treat someone or something cruelly, roughly, or abusively.
examples: The new laws punish anyone who maltreats animals.
The prisoners claimed they had been maltreated by their guards.

meanings.definition: An additional remark added to a letter after the writer's signature, often marked P.S.
examples: She added a postscript inviting him to visit that summer.
In a hasty postscript he mentioned the change of date.

meanings.definition: The science and practice of preparing and dispensing medicines.
examples: He studied pharmacy at the university for four years.

meanings.definition: A shop or place where medicines are prepared and sold.
examples: I picked up my prescription at the pharmacy on the corner.

meanings.definition: To stir up mild anger or resentment in someone.
examples: Her dismissive remark piqued his pride.

meanings.definition: To arouse interest or curiosity.
examples: The mysterious title piqued my curiosity at once.

meanings.definition: To obtain or reach a conclusion by reasoning from a source or premise.
examples: She derived the formula from a few basic principles.
Many English words are derived from Latin roots.

meanings.definition: To deprive of natural simplicity, making more worldly or complex.
examples: Years in the city had sophisticated his once-rustic tastes.

meanings.definition: A worldly, cultured, and refined person.
examples: She moved through the gallery like a true sophisticate.

meanings.definition: A king or prince of high rank in India.
examples: The maharaja ruled over a vast and wealthy province.
Tourists marveled at the maharaja's gilded palace.

meanings.definition: Able to be used up or completely depleted.
examples: Fossil fuels are an exhaustible resource that will eventually run out.
Her patience, though great, was clearly exhaustible.

meanings.definition: A temple dedicated to all the gods, especially the domed temple in Rome.
examples: We admired the great dome of the Pantheon in Rome.

meanings.definition: All the gods of a people, or a group of especially famous or important people.
examples: He earned a place in the pantheon of jazz greats.

meanings.definition: To regard with deep respect and reverence.
examples: Students revered the old professor for his wisdom.
The poet is revered throughout his homeland.

meanings.definition: The act of deciding or ordaining something in advance; predestination.
examples: He believed that foreordination governed every event in his life.
The sermon explored the doctrine of divine foreordination.

meanings.definition: A feeling of trust or reliance in someone or something.
examples: The team placed great confidence in their new captain.

meanings.definition: Belief in one's own abilities or qualities; self-assurance.
examples: She answered every question with calm confidence.

meanings.definition: A secret or private matter told in trust.
examples: He shared the news with her in strict confidence.

meanings.definition: Involving or constituting betrayal of one's country or sovereign.
examples: The pamphlet was condemned as treasonable propaganda.
He was arrested for making treasonable statements against the crown.

meanings.definition: Showing intense and passionate feeling.
examples: She made a fervent plea for peace.
His fervent admirers waited for hours to meet him.

meanings.definition: To make drunk with alcohol.
examples: A single glass of strong wine was enough to intoxicate him.

meanings.definition: To excite or elate beyond self-control, as with strong emotion.
examples: The roar of the crowd seemed to intoxicate the young singer.

meanings.definition: Existence at a time before something else.
examples: The philosopher argued for the soul's preexistence before birth.
She questioned the preexistence of the universe.

meanings.definition: A clear and detailed explanation or presentation of an idea or theory.
examples: The book offers a lucid exposition of quantum theory.

meanings.definition: A large public exhibition of art or industry.
examples: The world's exposition drew visitors from every continent.

meanings.definition: So absurd or ridiculous as to invite laughter.
examples: His excuse for being late was utterly ludicrous.
They wore ludicrous costumes to the party.

meanings.definition: Perfectly clean, neat, or free from flaw.
examples: The kitchen was immaculate, with not a crumb in sight.
She kept an immaculate record throughout her career.

meanings.definition: To form a broad conclusion or statement from particular instances.
examples: It is unfair to generalize about a whole nation from one visit.
From these few results she generalized a wider principle.

meanings.definition: A person or organization competing against others, such as a rival.
examples: The runner outpaced every competitor in the race.
The company undercut its competitors on price.

meanings.definition: The act of making known something previously secret or unknown.
examples: The revelation of the scandal shocked the entire town.

meanings.definition: A surprising and previously unknown fact that is disclosed.
examples: It was a revelation to learn how the trick was done.

meanings.definition: To collect or form into a cluster or mass.
examples: Dust particles agglomerated into larger clumps.
The company agglomerated several smaller firms into one.

meanings.definition: To give someone the authority or power to do something.
examples: The new law empowers citizens to challenge unfair fees.
Education empowers people to improve their own lives.

meanings.definition: Making little or no noise.
examples: The library was so quiet you could hear a pin drop.
She spoke in a quiet voice so as not to wake the baby.

meanings.definition: Calm, peaceful, and without much activity.
examples: They spent a quiet evening reading by the fire.

meanings.definition: Given to arguing or disputing; easily angered.
examples: The quarrelsome neighbors argued over every little thing.
He grew quarrelsome whenever he was tired.

meanings.definition: The state of being held in low regard; a bad reputation.
examples: The scandal brought the firm into disrepute.
His careless work fell into disrepute among his peers.

meanings.definition: Resembling ashes in color; very pale, especially from fear or illness.
examples: Her face turned ashen when she heard the terrible news.
His ashen complexion worried the doctors.

meanings.definition: To make something clear or intelligible, as if by shedding light on it.
examples: A single example can irradiate an obscure idea.

meanings.definition: To expose to or treat with radiation or light.
examples: The samples were irradiated to kill any bacteria.

meanings.definition: A lever operated by the foot on a machine, vehicle, or musical instrument.
examples: She pressed the pedal and the piano note rang out softly.
He pushed harder on the bicycle pedal to climb the hill.

meanings.definition: Shaking or quivering slightly, often from nervousness or weakness.
examples: She answered in a tremulous voice, close to tears.
His tremulous hands could barely hold the cup.

meanings.definition: The application of scientific knowledge to industry and practical purposes.
examples: Advances in technology have transformed how we communicate.
The factory invested in the latest manufacturing technology.

meanings.definition: In Greek myth, a sea-nymph whose enchanting song lured sailors to their deaths.
examples: The siren's song was said to draw ships onto the rocks.

meanings.definition: A device that produces a loud warning sound.
examples: An ambulance sped past with its siren wailing.

meanings.definition: A dark reddish-brown nonmetallic chemical element that is liquid at room temperature and has a sharp, suffocating odor.
examples: Bromine fumes are toxic and must be handled in a fume hood.
The chemist measured out a few drops of bromine.

meanings.definition: To fill someone with horror or dismay.
examples: The cruelty of the act appalled everyone who heard of it.
She was appalled by the mess they had left behind.

meanings.definition: To make a copy or representation of something.
examples: The printer can reproduce the photograph in fine detail.
He tried to reproduce the painting stroke by stroke.

meanings.definition: To produce offspring; to breed.
examples: Rabbits reproduce very quickly.

meanings.definition: The brutal and indiscriminate killing of many people.
examples: The village was the site of a terrible massacre.

meanings.definition: To kill many people brutally and indiscriminately.
examples: The invaders massacred the defenseless inhabitants.

meanings.definition: The system or process of draining away water or waste liquid.
examples: Poor drainage left the field flooded after every storm.
Engineers improved the city's drainage to prevent flooding.

meanings.definition: About to happen very soon; impending.
examples: Dark clouds warned that a storm was imminent.
The company faced the imminent threat of bankruptcy.

meanings.definition: A harsh, inharmonious combination of sounds; discord.
examples: The dissonance of the clashing chords made listeners wince.

meanings.definition: A lack of agreement or harmony between things.
examples: There was a clear dissonance between his words and his actions.

meanings.definition: A word that sounds the same as another but differs in meaning and often spelling.
examples: "Their" and "there" are common homophones.
The teacher gave examples of homophones to avoid confusion.

meanings.definition: To cause or give rise to a feeling, situation, or condition.
examples: His honesty engendered trust among his colleagues.
Such policies only engender resentment.

meanings.definition: Extremely bad, abominable, or detestable.
examples: The food at the inn was execrable.
Critics called his behavior execrable.

meanings.definition: Enjoying the company of others; friendly and inclined to socialize.
examples: She is a sociable person who makes friends easily.
The party put everyone in a sociable mood.

meanings.definition: A regular payment made to a person, typically after retirement, for past service.
examples: After forty years of work he retired on a comfortable pension.
The company contributes to each employee's pension.

meanings.definition: The ability to produce a desired or intended result.
examples: Trials confirmed the efficacy of the new vaccine.
They doubted the efficacy of such a simple remedy.

meanings.definition: The formal giving up or rejection of a right, claim, or possession.
examples: His renunciation of the throne stunned the nation.
The treaty required the renunciation of all territorial claims.

meanings.definition: The quality of being uniform or composed of similar parts throughout.
examples: The homogeneity of the mixture ensured an even result.
Critics noted a dull homogeneity in the new suburb's houses.

meanings.definition: A title given to Jesus, meaning the anointed one or Messiah.
examples: Christians regard Christ as the savior of humankind.
The painting depicts Christ surrounded by his disciples.

meanings.definition: The act of stealing goods violently, especially during war.
examples: The city suffered weeks of pillage after it fell.

meanings.definition: To rob a place using violence, especially in wartime.
examples: The invading army pillaged every village in its path.

meanings.definition: A person who sells small goods, often pushily or by aggressive promotion.
examples: A huckster on the corner hawked cheap watches to passersby.
Critics dismissed him as a mere huckster of false cures.

meanings.definition: To take the place of something that is older or no longer effective.
examples: The new model supersedes last year's version entirely.
Email has largely superseded the handwritten letter.

meanings.definition: Relating to or resembling a dog.
examples: The trainer studied canine behavior for many years.
The dog's canine loyalty was unmistakable.

meanings.definition: A pointed tooth between the incisors and premolars.
examples: The dentist noted that one canine was slightly chipped.

meanings.definition: A musical composition in which the main theme returns repeatedly between contrasting sections.
examples: The sonata ends with a lively rondo.
She practiced the rondo until the recurring theme was flawless.

meanings.definition: A modern revival or reinvention of ancient pagan religious practices.
examples: Neopaganism draws on old nature-worshipping traditions.
The festival celebrated the rituals of modern neopaganism.

meanings.definition: The symbol &, standing for the word "and."
examples: The firm's name uses an ampersand between the two partners' surnames.
She replaced the word "and" with an ampersand to save space.

meanings.definition: The standard unit used to measure the strength of an electric current.
examples: The circuit breaker tripped because the load drew more than fifteen amperes.
A current of one ampere flows when one coulomb passes a point each second.

meanings.definition: The quality of being in accordance with the law or with accepted rules.
examples: The court questioned the legitimacy of the contract.
Voters doubted the legitimacy of the disputed election results.

meanings.definition: The condition of being born to legally married parents.
examples: The will hinged on proving the child's legitimacy.

meanings.definition: Having the smooth, rich texture or appearance of cream.
examples: She stirred the pot until the soup was thick and creamy.
The lotion left her skin feeling soft and creamy.

meanings.definition: Unwilling to accept views, beliefs, or behavior that differ from one's own.
examples: He was intolerant of anyone who disagreed with him.
An intolerant attitude has no place in a diverse classroom.

meanings.definition: Unable to endure a particular thing without harm or discomfort.
examples: Many adults are intolerant of lactose.

meanings.definition: A person or thing of enormous size, power, or importance.
examples: The tech company grew into a colossus that dominated the industry.
The ancient statue stood as a colossus over the harbor.

meanings.definition: To spread over or through something in large numbers, often causing damage.
examples: Weeds quickly overran the abandoned garden.
Invading troops overran the border towns within days.

meanings.definition: To exceed an expected or allowed limit, such as time or budget.
examples: The meeting overran by half an hour.

meanings.definition: To protest or complain bitterly and at length against something.
examples: The senator inveighed against the proposed tax increase.
Critics inveighed against the corruption of the regime.

meanings.definition: To clear someone of blame or accusation.
examples: New evidence exonerated the man after years in prison.
The committee's report exonerated her of any wrongdoing.

meanings.definition: A figure of speech in which something is referred to by the name of a closely associated attribute or thing.
examples: Using 'the crown' to mean the monarchy is an example of metonymy.
In the phrase 'the White House announced,' metonymy stands in for the president's staff.

meanings.definition: To walk or move about, especially on foot.
examples: After surgery, the patient was encouraged to ambulate slowly down the hall.
The nurse helped the elderly man ambulate to the window.

meanings.definition: To get back or demand the return of something that was lost or taken.
examples: She went to the office to reclaim her confiscated phone.
The team fought to reclaim the title they had lost last year.

meanings.definition: To make wasteland or flooded land suitable for use again.
examples: Engineers reclaimed the marsh and turned it into farmland.

meanings.definition: The branch of physics that deals with the motion of bodies and the forces acting on them.
examples: She studied mechanics to understand how planets orbit the sun.
Newton laid the foundations of classical mechanics.

meanings.definition: The practical details or working parts of how something operates.
examples: He explained the mechanics of filing a tax return.

meanings.definition: The use of flattery or coaxing speech to persuade someone, often deceptively.
examples: No amount of cajolery could change the judge's mind.
Through sheer cajolery he talked his way into the exclusive event.

meanings.definition: To tell a story or give an account of events.
examples: The author narrates the novel from a child's point of view.
A famous actor was hired to narrate the documentary.

meanings.definition: Much smaller than the usual or normal size.
examples: The collector displayed a miniature model of the sailing ship.
She bought a miniature poodle that fit in her bag.

meanings.definition: To stick out beyond a surface or edge.
examples: A nail protruded dangerously from the old board.
His ribs protruded after weeks without proper food.

meanings.definition: The state of being well known for a bad reputation or shameful act.
examples: The traitor's name lived on in infamy.
The scandal brought lasting infamy to the once-respected firm.

meanings.definition: The science that describes and maps the general features of the universe and the earth.
examples: Renaissance scholars studied cosmography to chart the heavens and the seas.
His treatise on cosmography combined astronomy with geography.

meanings.definition: Self-restraint in indulging one's desires, appetites, or passions.
examples: The monks practiced strict continence in food and pleasure.
Years of continence had given him remarkable discipline.

meanings.definition: The ability to control the bladder and bowels.
examples: The therapy helped the patient regain continence after surgery.

meanings.definition: Not allowing something, especially liquid, to pass through.
examples: The jacket is impervious to rain.
The new coating makes the wood impervious to water.

meanings.definition: Unable to be affected or influenced by something.
examples: She seemed impervious to criticism.

meanings.definition: A long narrative poem that celebrates the deeds of heroes and great events in formal, elevated verse.
examples: Homer's 'Odyssey' is one of the greatest epics ever written.
The class studied an ancient epic about a warrior's long journey home.

meanings.definition: Heroic or impressively great in scale or scope.
examples: They embarked on an epic journey across the continent.

meanings.definition: The amount of land measured in acres, especially land used for farming.
examples: The farm's acreage had doubled over the past decade.
They planted corn across the entire acreage.

meanings.definition: To set or fix something deeply and firmly, as if grafting it in.
examples: Her parents ingrafted a love of reading in her from an early age.
Customs ingrafted over centuries are hard to uproot.

meanings.definition: A person who is legally entitled to receive money, property, or other benefits.
examples: She named her daughter as the sole beneficiary of the life insurance policy.
The charity was the main beneficiary of his estate.

meanings.definition: Excessively concerned with one's clothes and refined manners.
examples: His foppish wardrobe drew amused glances at the office.
The character is a foppish dandy who fusses over every detail of his appearance.

meanings.definition: To overwhelm someone or something with a great amount of things.
examples: The office was inundated with applications after the ad ran.
Fans inundated the singer with messages of support.

meanings.definition: To cover an area with a flood of water.
examples: The river burst its banks and inundated the valley.

meanings.definition: A man with strong or excessive sexual desires.
examples: The novel's villain is portrayed as a shameless satyr.
Gossips painted him as an aging satyr chasing every woman in town.

meanings.definition: In Greek mythology, a woodland creature that is part man and part goat.
examples: A grinning satyr was carved into the temple frieze.

meanings.definition: A strong liking or inclination for something.
examples: She has a penchant for expensive shoes.
His penchant for taking risks eventually paid off.

meanings.definition: A state of stillness, calm, or inactivity.
examples: A deep quiescence settled over the village at dusk.
After the storm, the sea returned to quiescence.

meanings.definition: Containing or based on a mistake; incorrect.
examples: The report was withdrawn because of its erroneous figures.
He drew an erroneous conclusion from incomplete data.

meanings.definition: The length of time that something lasts or continues.
examples: The duration of the flight was about six hours.
Visitors must wear a badge for the duration of their stay.

meanings.definition: A good reason or explanation that shows something to be right or reasonable.
examples: There was no justification for such rudeness.
He offered a detailed justification for the budget cuts.

meanings.definition: The system or process of transmitting speech and sound over a distance by telephone.
examples: Internet telephony has largely replaced traditional phone lines.
The company specializes in business telephony systems.

meanings.definition: An upright bar, post, or frame used as a support or to confine cattle in a stall.
examples: The cow was secured in the stanchion for milking.
Heavy stanchions held up the ship's railing.

meanings.definition: Having many branches; branch-like in form.
examples: The coral grew in a ramose pattern across the reef.
Botanists noted the plant's ramose stems.

meanings.definition: Able to be understood; clear.
examples: His handwriting was barely intelligible.
She gave an intelligible explanation that even beginners could follow.

meanings.definition: To carry out or accomplish a task or action.
examples: The surgeon performed the operation flawlessly.
The software can perform thousands of calculations per second.

meanings.definition: To present a play, piece of music, or other entertainment to an audience.
examples: The band performed to a sold-out crowd.

meanings.definition: To officially choose or appoint someone or something for a particular purpose.
examples: The committee designated her as the new chairperson.
This area has been designated a wildlife sanctuary.

meanings.definition: To move back or gradually away from a previous point.
examples: The floodwaters slowly receded after the rain stopped.
As the ship sailed on, the shoreline receded into the distance.

meanings.definition: Friendly and peaceful relations between people or nations.
examples: The two countries signed a treaty to preserve amity.
Years of amity bound the neighboring villages together.

meanings.definition: A sheath that holds and protects the blade of a sword or knife.
examples: The knight slid his sword into its scabbard.
The dagger's jeweled scabbard hung at his belt.

meanings.definition: A musical composition written for one or more solo instruments accompanied by an orchestra.
examples: She performed a piano concerto by Mozart.
The violin concerto received a standing ovation.

meanings.definition: To take away from the value, quality, or reputation of something.
examples: The cheap frame detracted from the beauty of the painting.
Nothing could detract from her achievement.

meanings.definition: Relating to money or the management of money.
examples: The company faced serious financial difficulties.
She sought financial advice before investing.

meanings.definition: A lack of seriousness, especially when seriousness is expected.
examples: The judge frowned at the levity in the courtroom.
A moment of levity lightened the tense meeting.

meanings.definition: Having a long-established and firmly fixed habit that is unlikely to change.
examples: He is an inveterate gambler who cannot resist the tables.
She was an inveterate optimist, even in hard times.

meanings.definition: To make a bad or unpleasant situation better.
examples: New medication helped ameliorate the patient's symptoms.
The charity works to ameliorate the conditions of the poor.

meanings.definition: The point or place where something begins or comes from.
examples: The origin of the custom is lost in history.
Scientists traced the origin of the disease to a single source.

meanings.definition: Causing a strong feeling of disgust or distaste.
examples: The idea of cheating was repugnant to her.
He found the smell utterly repugnant.

meanings.definition: A weather system of high atmospheric pressure with air flowing outward, usually bringing calm, settled weather.
examples: An anticyclone over the region brought clear skies all week.
Forecasters predicted that the anticyclone would keep temperatures high.

meanings.definition: To do, use, or perform something to excess.
examples: Don't overdo the exercise on your first day back.
She tends to overdo the spices in her cooking.

meanings.definition: To cook food for too long.
examples: He overdid the steak until it was tough and dry.

meanings.definition: To succeed in doing or completing something.
examples: She accomplished her goal of running a marathon.
The team accomplished the project ahead of schedule.

meanings.definition: A lack of trust or confidence in someone or something.
examples: Years of broken promises bred deep distrust.
There was open distrust between the rival factions.

meanings.definition: To have little trust or confidence in someone or something.
examples: She distrusted his motives from the start.

meanings.definition: Not showing or feeling emotion; unaffected by feeling.
examples: His impassible expression revealed nothing of his thoughts.
She remained impassible even as the bad news was read aloud.

meanings.definition: A salt or ester of acetic acid.
examples: Sodium acetate is used in some chemical hand warmers.
The lab prepared a solution of lead acetate.

meanings.definition: Based on or producing an illusion; deceptive.
examples: The promise of easy wealth proved illusive.
An illusive calm hid the danger that lay ahead.

meanings.definition: An official who performs a particular function or duty, especially a minor one.
examples: A government functionary stamped each form in turn.
The party functionaries arranged the rally.

meanings.definition: To make something no longer legally or logically valid.
examples: A single error could invalidate the entire contract.
The new evidence invalidated their earlier conclusion.

meanings.definition: Serving to disconnect, separate, or mark a contrast between things.
examples: The word 'or' is a disjunctive conjunction.
The two clauses are joined in a disjunctive relationship.

meanings.definition: To make a problem or bad situation worse.
examples: The drought only exacerbated the food shortage.
His harsh words exacerbated the tension in the room.
